title: The 6 Claude Features to Get You Ahead of 99% of People using AI
summary: Tutorial covering six Claude products and features including Claude.ai, desktop app, mobile interface, and API access for building AI agents. Highlights privacy controls and data handling. Emphasizes that most users leverage only a fraction of Claude's full capabilities across writing, thinking, research, and task automation.
writeup: A structured tour of six distinct Claude interfaces, from the familiar Claude.ai chat to the agent-tier desktop tools, organized into four usage modes and two capability add-ons. The through-line is that most users are locked into the chat surface while agentic tools such as Claude Code and Claude Co-work operate on real files and build real software without requiring coding knowledge. The video targets non-developers who have heard of Claude but have not yet adopted its autonomous, computer-controlling modes.
key_points: Claude Co-work is a desktop application that reads, edits, and saves files on the user's machine, enabling fully delegated tasks such as summarizing and drafting email replies, organizing desktop files, or assembling a shopping list into a PDF, without the user writing code.

Claude Dispatch separates control from execution: a task is assigned from a phone and Claude performs it on a home or office desktop, with the resulting thread remaining continuous across devices so follow-up can happen from a laptop.

Claude Code writes, tests, and debugs complete applications inside the terminal or the desktop app; the presenter reports building over 150 AI agents through natural-language instructions alone, framing coding ability as no longer a prerequisite.
Plugins function as installable connectors (a Slack plugin, a design plugin) that extend Co-work and Code with new capabilities without the user building any integration themselves.

Skills are instruction folders that Claude reads every time a recurring job is triggered, eliminating the need to retype custom instructions for repeated tasks; they work across chat, Co-work, and Code after a single installation.

The presenter's taxonomy treats chat as the thinking layer, Co-work as the file and automation layer, Dispatch as the remote delegation layer, and Code as the software-building layer, with plugins and skills as cross-cutting capability multipliers.
